mappings.rationale: A core text on economic growth in developing countries, GDP per capita, the Solow growth model, and development planning (A.4, B.4).
Economic growth theory, productivity, human capital, and the relationship between growth and development are central themes (A.1, A.2).
Traces historical patterns of economic growth, divergence across countries, and the institutional causes of development (A, A.4.b).
Technology and innovation as drivers of economic growth -- including creative destruction and productivity gains -- are examined (A.1, A.5).
The pivotal period when technology, capital, and labour drove sustained economic growth is the central subject (A.1).
Explores how capital supply, market forces, and industrialization drive growth in market economies (A.1, B.3).
Economic planning in Communist countries and centrally planned growth models are examined (B.2).
Socialist approaches to economic planning and state-directed growth strategies are explored (B.2, B.3).
Keynesian demand-determined growth models and government fiscal policies for economic stability are examined (A.2.a, B.3).
Supply-side growth theories, market-oriented reform, and debates about economic planning are addressed (A.2.a, B.3).
The environmental costs of economic growth and market-based policy instruments for sustainable development are explored (A.3).
Social costs and benefits of economic growth -- including tipping points and sustainable development -- are central themes (A.3).
Malthusian theories of diminishing returns, population growth versus economic capacity, and economic retardation remain influential (A.4.a, A.4.b).
Smith's foundational theories of the wealth of nations, free trade as a growth driver, and productivity are examined (A.1, A.5).
Marxist theories of capital accumulation, economic development, and the dynamics of growth remain foundational (A.2, A.4.b).
A case study of economic planning and growth strategies in a formerly Communist state transitioning to a mixed economy (B.2, B.3).
Economic growth strategies in major developing countries, including their development planning, are examined (A.4, B.4).
Global economic integration and its effects on growth patterns and development opportunities across countries are explored (A.4).
Examines environmental inequality, climate justice, and the disproportionate impact of pollution on low-income communities, relevant to A.1 (social costs of growth) and B.2 (development planning).
